assertions.description: The schematic has two 'UVcc' text labels (at schematic positions near the USB connector and near U1's UVcc pin) that should form a named net connecting U1 pin 2 (UVcc power_in) to the USB circuitry. The analyzer places U1 pin 2 on '__unnamed_10' with no other connections, indicating the net label-to-pin matching failed for this custom library symbol. The UVcc net is used in the design to connect USB Vbus regulation; an isolated UVcc power pin is an incorrect representation of the schematic.

Because the custom keyboard_parts:ATMEGA32U4 symbol has non-standard pin positions, the analyzer's geometric wire-to-pin matching incorrectly assigns several I/O and power pins to the wrong nets. Examples: pin 16 (XTAL1) is on VCC, pin 21 (PD3/TXD1) is on VCC, pin 23 (GND) is on VCC, pin 18 (PD0/INT0) is on GND, pin 24 (AVCC) is on GND, pin 44 (AVCC) is on GND. This produces incorrect power domain analysis and false connections between unrelated signals.

INCORRECT: K1, K2, K3, K4 use the keyboard_parts:KEYSW symbol which is a 2-pin mechanical keyboard switch. The analyzer classifies all four as type 'relay', which inflates component_types.relay to 4 and undercounts component_types.switch (only SW1, the reset button, is correctly typed as switch). The lib_id 'keyboard_parts:KEYSW' is a switch, not a relay.

INCORRECT: Because K1-K4 are misclassified as relay, component_types.switch reports only 1 (just SW1). The correct count should be 5 if KEYSW is treated as a switch, or at minimum the relay bucket should be 0 and a more appropriate type used for KEYSW.

The schematic contains a textbook keyboard matrix: 4 switches (K1-K4) each paired with a series diode (D1-D4) organized on nets row0, row1 (diode anodes) and col0, col1 (switch columns), all driven by ATmega32U4 GPIO pins PD6/T1=row0, PD7/T0=row1, PB4=col0, PB5=col1. The signal_analysis.key_matrices array is empty. This is a clear false negative.
